title: Towards Efficient and Explainable HYDRA for Time Series Classification
description: This work improves the practical efficiency and interpretability of HYDRA, a state-of-the-art time series classification method. The paper introduces dynamic batching to reduce runtime on CPU and Apple MPS backends, and develops a HYDRA-specific saliency method for identifying decision-relevant temporal regions. The approach is evaluated across the UCR 128 archive using runtime benchmarks, accuracy comparisons, perturbation tests and a WindowSHAP comparison.
highlights: Reproduced and evaluated HYDRA and MrSQM across the full UCR 128 time series classification archive against logistic regression and random forest baselines.
Introduced length-aware dynamic batching, reducing HYDRA mean runtime from 6.54s to 2.13s on Apple MPS with no meaningful loss in accuracy.
Validated the batching strategy across consumer CPU/MPS backends and the current aeon implementation of HYDRA.
Developed a HYDRA-specific saliency projection that maps classifier evidence back through HYDRA's dilated convolutional feature space.
Evaluated saliency faithfulness using perturbation tests, morphology-based analysis and comparison against a WindowSHAP-style baseline.
Showed that HYDRA saliency provides a lower-cost model-specific relevance ranking, running approximately 6.2x faster per sample than WindowSHAP.
